section.title: Samson and Delilah
section.sectionRationale: The Samson story reaches its tragic climax and its startling grace. He escapes a trap at Gaza, then falls to Delilah, who wears down his heart until he betrays the secret of his Nazirite hair. Shaved, he loses the Spirit without knowing it, is blinded, bound, and set to grinding in prison. But his hair grows back, and in his final prayer God gives strength once more, so that in his death he kills more than in his life, pulling down Dagon's temple. One movement holds his ruin and his last, redemptive act together.
section.thesis: Samson's appetite finally masters him, and betraying his consecration he loses the LORD, his sight, and his freedom; yet God does not abandon him, and hearing his last humble prayer, grants a dying deliverer a final victory that shames the idol and rescues by death.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the escape from Gaza, verses 1 to 3; Delilah's price and the three deceptions, verses 4 to 14; the secret betrayed and the Spirit departed, verses 15 to 20; the blinded prisoner at the mill, verses 21 to 22; and the last prayer that brings down Dagon's temple, verses 23 to 31.

verses.text: One day Samson went to Gaza, where he saw a prostitute and went in to spend the night with her.

When the Gazites heard that Samson was there, they surrounded that place and lay in wait for him all night at the city gate. They were quiet throughout the night, saying, “Let us wait until dawn; then we will kill him.”

But Samson lay there only until midnight, when he got up, took hold of the doors of the city gate and both gateposts, and pulled them out, bar and all. Then he put them on his shoulders and took them to the top of the mountain overlooking Hebron.

units.label: Delilah and the three deceptions
units.note: how you can be tied up

verses.text: Some time later, Samson fell in love with a woman in the Valley of Sorek, whose name was Delilah.

The lords of the Philistines went to her and said, “Entice him and find out the source of his great {{koach|strength}} and how we can overpower him to tie him up and subdue him. Then each one of us will give you eleven hundred shekels of silver.”
So Delilah said to Samson, “Please tell me the source of your great strength and how you can be tied up and subdued.”
Samson told her, “If they tie me up with seven fresh bowstrings that have not been dried, I will become as weak as any other man.”
So the lords of the Philistines brought her seven fresh bowstrings that had not been dried, and she tied him up with them.

While the men were hidden in her room, she called out, “Samson, the Philistines are here!” But he snapped the bowstrings like a strand of yarn seared by a flame. So the source of his strength remained unknown.
Then Delilah said to Samson, “You have mocked me and lied to me! Now please tell me how you can be tied up.”
He replied, “If they tie me up with new ropes that have never been used, I will become as weak as any other man.”

So Delilah took new ropes, tied him up with them, and called out, “Samson, the Philistines are here!” But while the men were hidden in her room, he snapped the ropes off his arms like they were threads.

Then Delilah said to Samson, “You have mocked me and lied to me all along! Tell me how you can be tied up.” He told her, “If you weave the seven braids of my head into the web of a loom and tighten it with a pin, I will become as weak as any other man.”

So while he slept, Delilah took the seven braids of his hair and wove them into the web. Then she tightened it with a pin and called to him, “Samson, the Philistines are here!” But he awoke from his sleep and pulled out the pin with the loom and the web.

units.label: The secret betrayed and the Spirit departed
units.note: he did not know that the LORD had departed

verses.text: “How can you say, ‘I love you,’” she asked, “when your heart is not with me? This is the third time you have mocked me and failed to reveal to me the source of your great strength!”
Finally, after she had pressed him daily with her words and pleaded until he was sick to death,

Samson told her all that was in his heart: “My hair has never been cut, because I have been a {{nazir|Nazirite}} to God from my mother’s womb. If I am shaved, my strength will leave me, and I will become as weak as any other man.”

When Delilah realized that he had revealed to her all that was in his heart, she sent this message to the lords of the Philistines: “Come up once more, for he has revealed to me all that is in his heart.” Then the lords of the Philistines came to her, bringing the money in their hands.
And having lulled him to sleep on her lap, she called a man to shave off the seven braids of his head. In this way she began to subdue him, and his strength left him.

Then she called out, “Samson, the Philistines are here!” When Samson awoke from his sleep, he thought, “I will escape as I did before and shake myself free.” But he did not know that the LORD had {{sur|departed}} from him.

units.label: Blinded at the mill
units.note: the hair began to grow back

verses.text: Then the Philistines seized him, gouged out his eyes, and brought him down to Gaza, where he was bound with bronze shackles and forced to grind grain in the prison.
However, the hair of his head began to grow back after it had been shaved.

units.label: The last prayer and Dagon's fall
units.note: let me die with the Philistines

verses.text: Now the lords of the Philistines gathered together to offer a great sacrifice to their god Dagon. They rejoiced and said, “Our god has delivered Samson our enemy into our hands.”
And when the people saw him, they praised their god, saying: “Our god has delivered into our hands our enemy who destroyed our land and multiplied our dead.”
And while their hearts were merry, they said, “Call for Samson to entertain us.” So they called Samson out of the prison to entertain them. And they stationed him between the pillars.
Samson said to the servant who held his hand, “Lead me where I can feel the pillars supporting the temple, so I can lean against them.”
Now the temple was full of men and women; all the lords of the Philistines were there, and about three thousand men and women were on the roof watching Samson entertain them.
Then Samson called out to the LORD: “O Lord GOD, please {{zakar|remember}} me. Strengthen me, O God, just once more, so that with one vengeful blow I may pay back the Philistines for my two eyes.”
And Samson reached out for the two central pillars supporting the temple. Bracing himself against them with his right hand on one pillar and his left hand on the other,

Samson said, “Let me die with the Philistines.” Then he pushed with all his might, and the temple fell on the lords and all the people in it. So in his death he killed more than he had killed in his life.
Then Samson’s brothers and his father’s family came down, carried him back, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of his father Manoah. And he had judged Israel twenty years.

koach.gloss: Strength, power, might. Delilah's whole scheme circles the secret of Samson's koach, his great strength, verses 5 and following. The narrative slowly exposes that this power was never his own possession but a gift bound to his consecration; when the sign of that consecration is gone, so is the strength, teaching that his might was always the LORD's to give and to withdraw.

nazir.gloss: One separated or consecrated, a Nazirite, vowed to God with signs including uncut hair. Samson confesses he has been a nazir from his mother's womb, verse 17. His uncut hair was the last visible mark of a consecration he had already violated in heart and deed; betraying it to Delilah is the final surrender of the calling he had long treated carelessly.

sur.gloss: To turn aside, depart, remove. Verse 20 gives the chapter's most sobering line: he did not know that the LORD had sur, departed, from him. The word marks the tragic moment when the Spirit's power lifts; Samson presumes on a grace he no longer has, warning that gifts can be presumed upon until, unnoticed, the Giver withdraws.

zakar.gloss: To remember, call to mind, act on behalf of. Samson's final prayer, please zakar me, verse 28, is a plea for God to act for him once more. In Scripture God's remembering is never mere recollection but covenant faithfulness moving to help; the blind, humbled deliverer casts himself on that mercy, and God answers, granting a final victory.

basic.context: Samson goes to Gaza, sleeps with a prostitute, and escapes an ambush by tearing off the city gates. Then he falls in love with Delilah, and the Philistine lords bribe her to discover the secret of his strength. Three times he lies and three times escapes, but she wears him down with daily pressure until, sick to death of her nagging, he tells her the truth: his strength is bound to his Nazirite consecration, marked by his uncut hair. She has his head shaved as he sleeps, and his strength leaves him. Most tragically, he does not know the LORD has departed. The Philistines gouge out his eyes and set him grinding grain in prison. But his hair grows back. At a great feast to Dagon, mocked as entertainment, Samson prays one last time for God to remember him, and pushing down the temple pillars, he kills more in his death than in his life.
basic.hermeneutics: This is tragedy told honestly, not a tale to imitate. Samson's lust, his careless treatment of his consecration, and his final cry for personal vengeance are all described, not commended. The uncut hair was never magic; it was the sign of a consecration that Samson had already trampled, and the real loss in verse 20 is the LORD Himself departing. Yet the chapter ends in grace: God hears a humbled, broken prayer and works one last deliverance, so we read Samson's ruin as warning and his end as mercy.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on four words. Delilah hunts the secret of Samson's koach, his strength, which proves to be God's gift, not his own. He confesses he is a nazir, a Nazirite consecrated from the womb, though he had long profaned it. The most terrible line is that the LORD had sur, departed, from him, while he did not even know it. And at the last, blind and humbled, he prays please zakar, remember, me, casting himself on God's covenant mercy, which answers.
basic.typology: Samson, the deliverer who dies destroying the enemies of God's people and accomplishes more in his death than in his life, faintly foreshadows Christ, who conquers through death. But the shadow is dim and marred: Samson dies for revenge and beside his sin, while Christ dies in love and without sin. The blinded, mocked deliverer between the pillars also recalls, at a distance, the mocked Saviour who pulls down the powers by His cross.
basic.christ: Samson accomplishes his greatest victory in his death, killing more than in all his life, and in this he dimly points to Christ, whose death is His triumph. But Christ is everything Samson is not. Samson is blinded by his own sin and prays for vengeance; Christ, though sinless, is mocked and killed, and prays for His enemies' forgiveness. Samson brings down a pagan temple in a burst of returning strength; Christ, by dying, destroys the true enemy and disarms every power. Where Samson kills to save, Christ dies to save, taking the judgment on Himself so that His death gives life rather than takes it.
basic.god: The chapter magnifies God's holiness and His grace together. He is holy: consecration cannot be trifled with, and when Samson surrenders the last sign of it, the LORD departs, and the strength goes with Him. Yet He is gracious: He does not cast Samson off forever. The quiet note that his hair began to grow back signals returning mercy, and God hears the blind prisoner's prayer, granting a final victory that exposes the impotence of Dagon. Even the humbling of His flawed servant serves God's purpose to save Israel and shame the idols.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 13 recounts Samson's Nazirite calling and uncut hair. 1 Samuel 5 shows Dagon falling before the ark, the impotence of the idol Samson's God overthrows. Hebrews 11 lists Samson among those who by faith were made strong out of weakness. And John 12 records Christ saying that unless a grain of wheat dies it remains alone, the death that brings much fruit.
basic.oneStory: Samson's tragic fall and his last, grace-given victory bring the judge cycle to its lowest and most poignant point. A consecrated deliverer ruins himself, yet God will not let His purpose fail; He hears a broken prayer and saves through the deliverer's death. It is the deepest cry yet in Judges for a Saviour who will not fail, pointing beyond Samson to Christ, who conquers not beside His sin but in perfect love, and kills death itself by dying.

context.literary: This is the climax and close of the Samson cycle, structured around repeated patterns: three failed attempts before the fatal fourth, echoing chapter 14's riddle. The tension builds through Delilah's daily pressing, mirroring the earlier wife's tears, and the narrator sharpens the tragedy with the line that Samson did not know the LORD had left him. The story turns on the pillars: the mocked, blind man at the center becomes the instrument of Dagon's fall, a reversal the reader feels keenly.
context.historical: Gaza and Ashkelon were among the five Philistine cities; the Valley of Sorek lay on the border near Samson's homeland. Dagon was a chief Philistine deity, associated with grain, so crediting him with capturing Samson was a public claim of the god's supremacy. The bribe of eleven hundred shekels of silver from each of the five lords was an enormous sum. Grinding grain in prison was degrading labor, often women's work, deepening Samson's humiliation, and temples with central supporting pillars are known from Philistine architecture.
context.canonical: Samson's end is the last and lowest of the major judges, a deliverer who saves only by dying and only after ruinous compromise, exposing Israel's desperate need for a true and faithful Saviour. The fall of Dagon's temple anticipates Dagon's collapse before the ark in 1 Samuel 5 and the wider biblical theme of the LORD's triumph over idols, while the deliverer who conquers through death points forward to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the Samson story to its tragic climax, exposing the ruin that follows a consecration treated carelessly, and yet to display God's persistent grace in hearing a humbled prayer and granting a final deliverance that shames the idol and saves Israel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Samson's immorality, his careless surrender of his consecration, and his final prayer for personal vengeance; it does not prescribe them. His lust and his revenge are warnings, not models. What endures is the seriousness of consecration, the peril of presuming on God's gifts, and the depth of God's grace toward a broken and repentant servant.

debates.question: Was Samson's uncut hair the actual source of his strength?

views.label: The hair was the sign, not the source; the LORD was the source
views.case: Verse 20 makes the real issue plain: the LORD had departed from him. The hair was the last visible mark of his Nazirite consecration, and cutting it signaled the final abandonment of that vow; the strength went because the Spirit went, not because hair holds power. God, not hair, was always the source.

views.label: The hair carried a real, God-appointed significance
views.case: God had tied the sign so closely to the vow that violating it genuinely triggered the loss; the hair was not magic, but as the God-given token of consecration its removal was the decisive breach that God had appointed to mark the withdrawal of His power.

debates.bearing: Both agree the power was God's; the hair mattered only as the sign of consecration. This guards us from superstition and presses the real lesson: the tragedy is losing the LORD, not losing hair.

debates.question: Is Samson's death an act of faith or merely a last act of vengeance?

views.label: A genuine act of faith, however mixed
views.case: Hebrews 11:32-34 commends Samson's faith, and his final prayer casts him wholly on God, please remember me, strengthen me; God answers, and through his death Israel is delivered. Beneath the language of vengeance is a real return to dependence on the LORD.

views.label: Faith still entangled with personal revenge
views.case: The prayer explicitly asks to pay back the Philistines for his two eyes, framed as vengeance; even at the end Samson's motives are mixed, and the scene is as much a warning about a wasted life as a triumph of faith.

debates.bearing: Both truths hold: God graciously receives and uses a flawed, mixed-motive prayer, so that the ending is real grace to an undeserving servant, warning us against wasting our calling while assuring us God hears the broken.

study.originalLanguages: Four words carry the tragedy and its grace. Delilah's whole scheme circles the secret of Samson's koach, his great strength, verses 5 and following; the narrative slowly reveals that this power was never a possession he controlled but a gift tied to his consecration. That consecration is named when he confesses he is a nazir, a Nazirite to God from his mother's womb, verse 17, though by now he had long profaned the vow in appetite and deed, and his uncut hair was its last outward sign. The chapter's most devastating line uses sur: he did not know that the LORD had departed from him, verse 20. The strength drained not because hair holds magic but because the Giver withdrew, and, most terrible of all, Samson did not even notice, so far had he drifted while presuming on grace. Yet the end turns on zakar. Blind, shackled, and mocked, Samson prays, O Lord GOD, please remember me, verse 28. In Scripture God's remembering is covenant mercy moving to act, and the humbled deliverer throws himself on that mercy. God answers: the strength returns once more, the temple falls, and Dagon is shamed. Strength, consecration, the departed LORD, and the God who remembers, the words trace a fall into ruin and a last, unmerited grace.

typology.type: The deliverer who accomplishes more in his death than in his life
typology.fulfillment: Christ, whose death is His victory, achieving in His dying the salvation His life pointed toward, John 12:24 and Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: The mocked, blinded deliverer between the pillars who topples the pagan temple
typology.fulfillment: Christ, mocked and killed, who by His cross pulls down the powers and shames every idol, 1 Corinthians 1:23-25.

typology.type: Strength restored to the humbled servant who calls on God
typology.fulfillment: Christ, raised in power after the weakness of the cross, and the pattern that God exalts the humble, Philippians 2:8-9.

study.christ: Samson wins his greatest victory in his death, killing more than in all his life, and in that he dimly foreshadows Christ, whose death is His triumph. But the likeness only sharpens the contrast. Samson is blinded by the fruit of his own sin; Christ, without sin, is stripped and mocked for ours. Samson prays for vengeance, to pay back the Philistines for his two eyes; Christ prays, Father, forgive them, Luke 23:34. Samson pulls down a temple of Dagon in a final surge of borrowed strength; Christ, by dying, disarms the true powers and principalities and makes a public spectacle of them, Colossians 2:15. Samson kills to save and dies beside his enemies in wrath; Christ dies for His enemies in love, and His death gives life rather than takes it. The broken shadow makes us long for the flawless Substance: a Deliverer who saves not despite His character but through His perfect obedience unto death.
study.god: The chapter holds God's holiness and His grace together without softening either. He is holy: consecration is not to be trifled with, and when Samson finally surrenders its last sign, the LORD departs, and the strength departs with Him, for the power was always God's to give and to withdraw. Yet the LORD is astonishingly gracious. He does not cast His failing servant off forever; the quiet clause that his hair began to grow back whispers of returning mercy, and when the blind prisoner cries out, God does not despise the broken, mixed prayer but answers it, granting a final deliverance. In that answer the LORD exposes Dagon as an empty idol, for the god who boasted of capturing Samson is buried under his own temple. God is faithful to His purpose to save Israel, faithful to a covenant His servant broke, and merciful to the one who at last remembers to call on Him.

crossReferences.note: Samson's Nazirite calling from the womb, with uncut hair as its sign, the consecration betrayed here.
Dagon falls before the ark of the LORD, the impotence of the idol Samson's God overthrows.
Samson named among those who by faith were made strong out of weakness, despite his failures.
Unless a grain of wheat falls and dies it remains alone, the death that bears much fruit, fulfilled in Christ.

study.oneStory: Samson's tragic fall and his final, grace-given victory bring the cycle of the judges to its lowest and most poignant point: a consecrated deliverer who ruins himself, loses the LORD without noticing, and yet is not cast off. God hears his broken prayer and saves through the deliverer's death, shaming the idol Dagon. It is the deepest cry in Judges for a Saviour who will not fail, and it points beyond Samson to Christ, who conquers not beside His sin but in perfect love, and who by dying destroys death itself and gives His people life.

quiz.q: How did Delilah finally discover the secret of Samson's strength?

options.text: She wore him down with daily pressure until, sick to death, he told her his strength was tied to his Nazirite consecration and uncut hair.
options.note: Verses 16-17 show the relentless pressing and his confession of the true secret.

options.text: She guessed it correctly on her first attempt.
options.note: It took three false answers and daily pressure before he told her the truth.

options.text: The Philistine lords told her the secret.
options.note: The lords did not know it; that is why they paid her to find it out.

options.text: Samson boasted about it openly to everyone.
options.note: He guarded the secret through three deceptions before Delilah pried it out.

quiz.q: What is the most tragic thing about Samson's loss of strength in verse 20?

options.text: He did not even know that the LORD had departed from him.
options.note: Verse 20 names the deepest tragedy: the LORD had left, and Samson was unaware.

options.text: His hair had been cut off while he slept.
options.note: The shaving was the sign, but the real loss was the LORD departing, unnoticed.

options.text: He was tied up with new ropes.
options.note: The ropes were an earlier deception; the true loss was the departure of the LORD.

options.text: He lost a wager to the Philistines.
options.note: That was chapter 14; here the tragedy is the LORD leaving him unawares.

quiz.q: What was the real source of Samson's strength?

options.text: The LORD Himself; the uncut hair was only the sign of a consecration he had betrayed.
options.note: Verse 20 shows the strength went because the LORD departed, not because hair holds power.

options.text: The hair itself, which held magical power.
options.note: The hair was the sign of his vow, not a magic source; the power was God's.

options.text: His own natural physical ability.
options.note: His strength was repeatedly credited to the Spirit of the LORD, not mere physique.

options.text: The bronze shackles he was bound with.
options.note: The shackles bound him in weakness; they were never a source of strength.

quiz.q: What did Samson pray at the end, and what happened?

options.text: He asked God to remember and strengthen him once more, and pushing the pillars he killed more in his death than in his life.
options.note: Verses 28-30 record his prayer and the final victory that toppled Dagon's temple.

options.text: He asked God to let him escape and live in peace.
options.note: He asked for strength for one last blow, not for escape; he said, let me die with the Philistines.

options.text: He cursed God for abandoning him.
options.note: He did the opposite: he humbly called on the LORD to remember him.

options.text: He prayed nothing and simply gave up.
options.note: He prayed earnestly, and God answered with a final deliverance.

quiz.q: How does Samson's death point to Christ, and how does it differ?

options.text: Both conquer through death, but Samson dies in vengeance beside his sin, while Christ dies sinless and in love, giving life rather than taking it.
options.note: Samson foreshadows Christ's victory-in-death by contrast; only Christ saves without sin and in love.

options.text: Samson's death shows we should seek revenge as he did.
options.note: His victory in death points to Christ, but his vengeful motive is a warning, not a model.

options.text: Samson's death proves that God abandons His servants for good.
options.note: The opposite: God heard his prayer and worked a final deliverance, showing grace, not abandonment.

options.text: Samson's death has no relation to Christ.
options.note: As a deliverer who saves through his death, he clearly, if dimly, foreshadows Christ.

questions: Samson gave away, piece by piece, the consecration meant to set him apart. Where are you slowly bargaining away something God has called you to guard?
The saddest line is that he did not know the LORD had departed from him. How can you keep from drifting so far that you presume on a grace you are no longer walking in?
Samson's strength was never his own; it was God's gift tied to his consecration. What gifts or blessings are you tempted to treat as your own possession rather than as trusts from God?
Blind, shackled, and mocked, Samson still cried, Lord, remember me, and God answered. What would it look like for you to bring a broken, humbled prayer to God today, trusting His mercy?
Samson conquered in death, but Christ conquers in death without sin and in love. How does seeing the flawless Deliverer behind the flawed one deepen your gratitude and your worship this week?

translationNotes.note: Eleven hundred shekels of silver from each Philistine lord was an enormous bribe; the BSB footnote notes 1,100 shekels is roughly 27.6 pounds or 12.5 kilograms of silver.

The clause about tightening the web with a pin follows the Septuagint and Vulgate; the BSB footnote records that the Hebrew does not include and tighten it with a pin, I will become as weak as any other man.
The opening of this verse also follows the Septuagint and Vulgate; the BSB footnote notes the Hebrew does not include the clause about Delilah weaving the braids while he slept.
She began to subdue him follows the Hebrew; the BSB footnote records that some Septuagint manuscripts read he began to grow weak, describing the same loss of strength.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 16:1-31. Poetic praise to Dagon joined inline with surrounding prose; interrupted quotations rejoined across footnote markers. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon set to none; no verified expository sermon on this passage confirmed this session.
